profile.summary: Leads the product function across one or more product areas, setting product strategy, prioritisation, and operating rhythm. Usually manages product managers and works closely with engineering, design, go-to-market leaders, and executives. Owns turning company goals into a coherent roadmap, making trade-offs across teams, and improving how product decisions get made.

known_challenges.text: Balancing long-term product strategy with constant pressure for short-term delivery and stakeholder asks.
Making hard prioritisation calls across teams with incomplete data and competing executive opinions.
Scaling product quality by coaching PMs instead of personally driving every important decision.
Carrying accountability for outcomes without direct control over engineering, design, sales, or marketing execution.
Sustaining energy under heavy context switching, decision load, and visible leadership pressure.

recommended_question_themes.why: This role is expected to connect company goals to product choices and explain what will not be done.
A senior Head of Product succeeds through stronger PM judgment, ownership, and consistency across the team.
Progress depends on productive working relationships with engineering, design, commercial teams, and executives.
They often have to make high-impact calls with imperfect data, so their decision process matters.
The role can become a constant stream of escalations, meetings, and trade-offs that erode focus and stamina.

terminology.meaning: Planned product direction and priorities over time.
The choices about where to play and why.
Deciding what gets attention and what waits.
Learning about user problems before building.
Goals and measurable outcomes for a period.
Key metrics used to track performance.
How a product is launched and sold.
Evidence the product solves a real market need.
Working with people who influence decisions and delivery.
Practices and support that improve product team effectiveness.

listen_for: Whether they talk mainly about outputs and launches or about customer and business outcomes.
How they describe trade-offs when executives or peers want conflicting things.
Whether they speak about PMs as owners they are growing or as work they still need to do themselves.
Shifts from strategic language to firefighting language, which can signal loss of control or overload.
How often they reference customer evidence versus internal opinion when explaining priorities.
avoid: Treating them like a senior individual PM instead of a functional leader building team judgment and product direction.
Pushing for certainty on roadmap commitments when the real job requires managing risk and trade-offs.
Assuming stakeholder conflict means poor collaboration rather than a normal part of the role.
Focusing only on delivery speed and ignoring whether the team is working on the right problems.
